title: Dog Licked Topical Pain Relief Cream: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog licked pain relief cream, it is a high-urgency emergency. Learn why NSAIDs like Diclofenac are toxic and how to help your dog now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingestion of human topical pain relief creams is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that can cause rapid kidney failure or internal ulcers. Seek immediate veterinary care if your dog licked or ate any amount of these products.

What happens if my dog licks or eats human topical pain relief cream?

Topical pain relief creams, such as those used for arthritis or muscle soreness, often contain active ingredients like Flurbiprofen, Diclofenac, Salicylates (found in products like Bengay or Icy Hot), or Lidocaine. While these are common in human medicine, they are processed differently by dogs. Even a small amount licked off a person's skin or directly from a tube can lead to severe poisoning.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my dog eats topical pain cream?

  • Urgency Level: High. This should be treated as an immediate veterinary emergency.
  • Ingredients like Flurbiprofen are extremely potent in canines; ingestion of even trace amounts can lead to rapid-onset kidney failure or life-threatening gastrointestinal ulceration.
  • Because damage can occur internally before you see physical symptoms, immediate professional intervention is required to prevent permanent organ damage.

How does taking a photo of the product packaging help the triage team?

  • When you call your veterinarian or an animal poison control hotline, they will need to know the exact concentration of the active ingredients.
  • Taking a clear photo of the front and back (the drug facts label) of the tube or packaging allows the triage team to calculate the potential toxic dose based on your dog's weight.
  • This information is vital for determining whether your dog needs aggressive hospitalization or outpatient decontamination.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of topical preparations by pets often results in mild gastroenteritis; however, certain agents such as 5-fluorouracil and calcipotriene can be fatal even at low doses. 5-Fluorouracil, available in ointments or topical solutions, is used to treat skin cancers and solar keratoses in people, and exposure in pets usually results from accidental ingestion. Lidocaine patches, while considered safe for topical use in dogs and cats due to low systemic absorption, can be toxic if orally ingested. Zinc oxide ointments or creams, commonly used as skin protectants, typically cause gastric irritation and diarrhea upon ingestion, with signs appearing within 2-4 hours of significant exposure.

Chapter: Toxicology, Dermatology, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3036)

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if my dog acts sick?
faqs.answer: No. Many toxins in pain creams cause silent internal damage to the kidneys and stomach lining. By the time a dog shows symptoms like vomiting or lethargy, significant damage may have already occurred.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically directed by a veterinarian. Depending on the ingredients and how long ago the ingestion occurred, inducing vomiting could cause further complications.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of pain cream toxicity?
faqs.answer: Common signs include vomiting (sometimes with blood), loss of appetite, abdominal pain, black or tarry stools, and increased thirst or urination.
